profile.name: Tobiloba Awolaju
profile.title: Web3 Systems Engineer • Smart Contract Developer • Multiplayer Game Architect
profile.bio: I build the systems most people are afraid to touch: real-time multiplayer sync, reorg-resistant blockchain indexers, and smart contracts that hold user funds. Currently finishing a degree in Electrical & Electronics Engineering at Adeleke University while building full-stack Web3 products solo — from Solidity to WebSocket servers to React frontends. I own the whole stack because I've had to.
profile.subtitle: Building on-chain games, DeFi protocols, and real-time decentralized applications.

projects.title: World of Nads ↗
projects.description: 20-player browser battle royale — 3D assets, a 20Hz WebSocket server keeping every player in sync in real time, on-chain prize distribution, and embedded wallet onboarding. No install, runs in-browser.

writeup.hardPart: Keeping 20 players' state perfectly synced over WebSocket while the frontend also has to talk to a wallet layer and on-chain state — three moving pieces that all had to stay consistent under real network jitter. I profiled and fixed a bottleneck where per-frame calls across the Godot/React bridge were tanking browser FPS from 60 to under 12, then rebuilt the sync path around batched state updates.
writeup.stack: Godot (WASM), TypeScript, React, Node.js WebSocket server, Solidity, PostgreSQL, Privy.

bullets: Built with React, Godot, Node.js, Solidity

details.overview: 20-player browser battle royale — 3D assets, a 20Hz WebSocket server keeping every player in sync in real time, on-chain prize distribution, and embedded wallet onboarding. No install, runs in-browser.
details.problem: Make a browser game feel native while synchronizing 20 players and settling rewards on-chain.
details.architecture: Godot runs in WebAssembly inside a React shell, player state moves through a Node.js WebSocket server, wallets connect through the frontend, and settlement is handled by Solidity contracts.
details.technicalChallenges: Keeping 20 players' state perfectly synced over WebSocket while the frontend also has to talk to a wallet layer and on-chain state — three moving pieces that all had to stay consistent under real network jitter. I profiled and fixed a bottleneck where per-frame calls across the Godot/React bridge were tanking browser FPS from 60 to under 12, then rebuilt the sync path around batched state updates.
details.solutions: Rebuilt the sync path around batched state updates and reduced per-frame calls across the Godot/React bridge.
details.techStack: Godot (WASM), TypeScript, React, Node.js WebSocket server, Solidity, PostgreSQL, Privy.
details.screenshots: World of Nads gameplay GIF sequence is used as lightweight project media.
details.lessonsLearned: Real-time Web3 games need clean boundaries between rendering, transport, wallet state, and contract settlement.

projects.title: MoyakiTraceEngine ↗
projects.description: A block indexer built for chains that reorganize. Cross-references three independent Monad nodes in real time and automatically rolls back state the moment it detects a parent-hash mismatch, so downstream consumers never read bad data.

writeup.hardPart: Public RPCs disagree with each other more than people expect. I designed a ReorgManager that treats disagreement as the normal case, not the exception — comparing state across nodes continuously and rolling back cleanly rather than crashing or silently serving stale data.
writeup.stack: Node.js, Express, Svelte, Firebase RTDB, EVM RPCs.

projects.title: Rug Pool ↗
projects.description: A memecoin launchpad where no one — not even the deployer — can exit early. On-chain, verifiable randomness decides who exits at the 24-hour mark; losers fund the winner's payout.

writeup.hardPart: Enforcing a hard lock at the contract level with no admin backdoor, while making the randomness both unpredictable and independently verifiable. Used Pyth Entropy for VRF. Shipped 5 contracts to Monad testnet with 53 passing tests and ran two full simulation cycles to confirm the payout logic held under edge cases.

details.overview: A memecoin launchpad where no one — not even the deployer — can exit early. On-chain, verifiable randomness decides who exits at the 24-hour mark; losers fund the winner's payout.
details.problem: Create a launch mechanism where early exit behavior is constrained by contracts rather than trust.
details.architecture: Protocol contracts enforce the lockup and payout flow, VRF supplies verifiable randomness, and the frontend exposes the game mechanics to users.
details.technicalChallenges: Enforcing a hard lock at the contract level with no admin backdoor, while making the randomness both unpredictable and independently verifiable. Used Pyth Entropy for VRF. Shipped 5 contracts to Monad testnet with 53 passing tests and ran two full simulation cycles to confirm the payout logic held under edge cases.
details.solutions: Kept the lock at contract level with no admin escape hatch and validated payout behavior through tests and simulation cycles.
details.screenshots: Project media is represented by the current preview asset until final screenshots are added.
details.lessonsLearned: Protocol UX is strongest when tokenomics, randomness, and safety guarantees are legible from the interface.
